content:
# Exodus Chapter 23 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~45v-46r (PDF pp. 89-90); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 65-66 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_023_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_023_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_023_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_023_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT NOTE -- CONCLUSION OF THE BOOK OF THE COVENANT:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 23 in standard prose column format with full Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. This chapter concludes the Book of the Covenant (*Sefer ha-Berit*) that began at 20:22, moving through four distinct sections: justice and integrity in legal proceedings (vv. 1-9), sabbatical rhythms of rest (vv. 10-13), the three pilgrimage festivals (vv. 14-19), and YHWH's promises concerning the angel, conquest, and covenant fidelity (vv. 20-33). The chapter transitions from casuistic and apodictic law into prophetic-promissory speech as YHWH shifts from legislator to warrior-guarantor. The Vaticanus manuscript (pp. 65-66) presents the LXX rendering in continuous uncial script across three columns per page; the LXX versification aligns with the MT throughout this chapter.

---

## Laws Concerning Justice and Integrity (vv. 1-3)

**23:1** You shall not lift up a report of emptiness. You shall not place your hand with the wicked to be a witness of violence. [fn1]

**23:2** You shall not follow after the many for evil, and you shall not testify upon a dispute to turn aside after the many to pervert justice. [fn2]

**23:3** And a poor man you shall not favor in his dispute. [fn3]

---

## Laws Concerning the Enemy's Animal (vv. 4-5)

**23:4** If you encounter the ox of your enemy or his donkey going astray, you shall surely return it to him. [fn4]

**23:5** If you see the donkey of one who hates you lying down under its burden, and you would refrain from loosening it for him -- you shall surely loosen it with him. [fn5]

---

## Justice for the Poor and the Sojourner (vv. 6-9)

**23:6** You shall not pervert the justice of your poor in his dispute. [fn6]

**23:7** From a word of falsehood keep far away, and the innocent and the righteous do not kill, for I will not declare righteous the wicked. [fn7]

**23:8** And a bribe you shall not take, for the bribe blinds the clear-sighted and perverts the words of the righteous. [fn8]

**23:9** And a sojourner you shall not oppress, for you know the soul of the sojourner, for sojourners you were in the land of Egypt. [fn9]

---

## The Sabbath Year and the Sabbath Day (vv. 10-13)

**23:10** And six years you shall sow your land and you shall gather in its yield. [fn10]

**23:11** And the seventh you shall release it and you shall abandon it, and the poor of your people shall eat, and their remainder the beast of the field shall eat. So you shall do with your vineyard and with your olive grove. [fn11]

**23:12** Six days you shall do your work, and on the seventh day you shall cease, so that your ox and your donkey may rest, and the son of your female servant and the sojourner may be refreshed. [fn12]

**23:13** And in all that I have said to you, you shall be watchful. And the name of other gods you shall not mention; it shall not be heard upon your mouth. [fn13]

---

## The Three Pilgrimage Festivals (vv. 14-19)

**23:14** Three times you shall make a pilgrimage-feast to me in the year. [fn14]

**23:15** The Feast of Unleavened Bread you shall keep: seven days you shall eat unleavened bread, as I commanded you, at the appointed time of the month of Aviv, for in it you went out from Egypt. And none shall appear before my face empty-handed. [fn15]

**23:16** And the Feast of Harvest, the firstfruits of your labors which you sow in the field; and the Feast of Ingathering at the going out of the year, when you gather in your labors from the field. [fn16]

**23:17** Three times in the year all your males shall appear before the face of the Lord YHWH. [fn17]

**23:18** You shall not slaughter upon leavened bread the blood of my sacrifice, and the fat of my feast shall not remain overnight until morning. [fn18]

**23:19** The first of the firstfruits of your ground you shall bring to the house of YHWH your God. You shall not boil a kid in the milk of its mother. [fn19]

---

## The Angel and the Conquest (vv. 20-26)

**23:20** Behold, I am sending an angel before you to guard you on the way and to bring you to the place which I have prepared. [fn20]

**23:21** Be watchful before him and listen to his voice; do not be rebellious against him, for he will not forgive your transgression, for my name is in his midst. [fn21]

**23:22** But if you surely listen to his voice and do all that I speak, then I will be an enemy to your enemies and an adversary to your adversaries. [fn22]

**23:23** For my angel shall go before you and bring you to the Amorite and the Hittite and the Perizzite and the Canaanite, the Hivite and the Jebusite, and I will annihilate them. [fn23]

**23:24** You shall not bow down to their gods and you shall not serve them, and you shall not do according to their deeds; but you shall surely tear them down and you shall surely break their pillars. [fn24]

**23:25** And you shall serve YHWH your God, and he will bless your bread and your water, and I will turn aside sickness from your midst. [fn25]

**23:26** There shall not be one who miscarries or one who is barren in your land; the number of your days I will fill. [fn26]

---

## The Conquest: Terror, Hornets, and Gradual Expulsion (vv. 27-33)

**23:27** My terror I will send before you, and I will throw into confusion all the people among whom you come, and I will give all your enemies to you as the back of the neck. [fn27]

**23:28** And I will send the hornet before you, and it will drive out the Hivite, the Canaanite, and the Hittite from before you. [fn28]

**23:29** I will not drive them out from before you in one year, lest the land become a desolation and the beast of the field multiply against you. [fn29]

**23:30** Little by little I will drive them out from before you, until you are fruitful and inherit the land. [fn30]

**23:31** And I will set your border from the Sea of Reeds and unto the Sea of the Philistines, and from the wilderness unto the River; for I will give into your hand the inhabitants of the land, and you shall drive them out from before you. [fn31]

**23:32** You shall not cut for them or for their gods a covenant. [fn32]

**23:33** They shall not dwell in your land, lest they cause you to sin against me, for if you serve their gods, surely it will be a snare to you. [fn33]

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- *Lo tissa shema shav al-tashet yadekha im-rasha lihyot ed khamas*: "You shall not lift up a report of emptiness. You shall not place your hand with the wicked to be a witness of violence"** -- EMET renders "you shall not lift up a report of emptiness...a witness of violence." LOGOS renders "you shall not raise a false report...a malicious witness." PNEUMA renders "you must not spread a false report...a malicious witness." The consensus follows EMET for both key phrases. *Shema shav* is "a report of emptiness" -- *shav* means "emptiness, falsehood, worthlessness" (the same word as in the Decalogue's *lashav*), and "emptiness" preserves its full semantic range better than LOGOS/PNEUMA's "false." For *ed khamas*, "a witness of violence" is preferred over LOGOS/PNEUMA's "malicious witness" because *khamas* ("violence, injustice") is a noun in construct, not an adjective -- the witness testifies toward violence, not merely with malice. EMET's "lift up" for *tissa* (from *nasa*, "to lift, carry") is preferred over LOGOS's "raise" and PNEUMA's "spread" as the most literal rendering of the verb. PNEUMA's "must not" is replaced by the standard "shall not" convention.

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Lo-tihyeh acharei-rabbim lera'ot velo-ta'aneh al-riv lintot acharei rabbim lehattot*: "You shall not follow after the many for evil, and you shall not testify upon a dispute to turn aside after the many to pervert justice"** -- EMET renders "you shall not be after the many for evil...you shall not answer upon a dispute." LOGOS renders "you shall not follow after the many for evil...you shall not testify concerning a dispute." PNEUMA renders "do not follow the crowd when they are doing wrong...do not go along with the majority to pervert justice." The consensus blends LOGOS's "follow" (which clarifies the unusual *lo-tihyeh*, literally "you shall not be") with EMET's "after the many" (preserving *acharei-rabbim*) and LOGOS's "testify" (a clearer rendering of *ta'aneh* in a judicial context than EMET's "answer"). PNEUMA's paraphrase ("the crowd," "go along with") is too free. The notoriously difficult syntax of this verse is preserved in its apparent twofold structure.

> **[fn3] v.3 -- *Vedal lo tehdar berivo*: "And a poor man you shall not favor in his dispute"** -- All three translators agree on substance: "a poor man you shall not favor in his dispute" (EMET), "a poor man you shall not favor in his dispute" (LOGOS), "do not show favoritism to a poor person in his lawsuit" (PNEUMA). The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA's "show favoritism" and "lawsuit" are unnecessarily dynamic. The Hebrew *tehdar* (from *hadar*, "to honor, show partiality") is well captured by "favor." This verse stands in deliberate tension with v. 6, forming a frame: no partiality toward the poor, no injustice against the poor.

> **[fn4] v.4 -- *Hashev teshivennu lo*: infinitive absolute** -- EMET renders "you shall surely return it to him." LOGOS renders "you shall surely return it to him." PNEUMA renders "you must bring it back to him." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS, preserving the infinitive absolute doubling (*hashev teshivennu*, "returning you shall return it") per binding convention. PNEUMA drops the emphatic construction. All three agree on "encounter" for *tifga* (qal of *paga*, "to meet, fall upon"). EMET/LOGOS preserve "your enemy" for *oyivkha*; PNEUMA uses "your enemy's" -- no substantive difference.

> **[fn5] v.5 -- *Azov ta'azov immo*: infinitive absolute and *khadal* clause** -- EMET renders "you would refrain from loosening it for him -- you shall surely loosen it with him." LOGOS renders "you shall refrain from leaving it to him; you shall surely release it with him." PNEUMA renders "do not walk past. You must help him get it back on its feet." The consensus follows EMET for "loosen" (rather than LOGOS's "release") because *azav* here carries the specific meaning of loosening a burden, and for the overall clause structure. EMET preserves the infinitive absolute (*azov ta'azov*, "loosening you shall loosen"). PNEUMA's paraphrase eliminates both the emphatic construction and the Hebrew vocabulary entirely. LOGOS's "leaving" for *me-azov* is acceptable but "loosening" better captures the contextual meaning of *azav* as releasing the animal from its burden rather than abandoning it.

> **[fn6] v.6 -- *Lo tatteh mishpat evyonekha berivo*: "You shall not pervert the justice of your poor in his dispute"** -- All three translators agree in substance. EMET and LOGOS render "you shall not pervert the justice of your poor in his dispute." PNEUMA renders "you must not deny justice to the poor among you in their lawsuits." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA's "deny" is weaker than "pervert" for *tatteh* (hiphil of *natah*, "to turn aside, bend"), and "lawsuits" is unnecessarily modern. Note the pairing of *dal* ("poor," v. 3) and *evyon* ("needy," v. 6) -- two different Hebrew words for poverty, framing the judicial section with complementary protections.

> **[fn7] v.7 -- *Middevar-sheqer tirchaq*: "from a word of falsehood keep far away"** -- EMET renders "from a word of falsehood you shall keep far." LOGOS renders "from a false matter keep far away." PNEUMA renders "stay far away from a false charge." The consensus follows a blend: "from a word of falsehood keep far away." EMET's "word of falsehood" preserves the Hebrew construct *devar-sheqer* (word/matter of falsehood) better than LOGOS's inversion to "false matter." LOGOS's "keep far away" is slightly more natural than EMET's "you shall keep far." PNEUMA's "false charge" is too narrow -- *devar-sheqer* is any word or matter of falsehood, not only a legal charge. For *lo atsdiq rasha*, "I will not declare righteous the wicked" (EMET) is preferred over LOGOS's "I will not justify the wicked" and PNEUMA's "I will not acquit the guilty" because the hiphil of *tsadaq* means specifically to declare righteous, and *rasha* is "wicked" not merely "guilty."

> **[fn8] v.8 -- *Piqqekhim*: "clear-sighted" vs. "seeing" vs. "those who see clearly"** -- EMET renders "the seeing." LOGOS renders "the clear-sighted." PNEUMA renders "those who see clearly." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "the clear-sighted" -- as the most precise rendering of *piqqekhim* (from *paqakh*, "to open the eyes"), which denotes those whose eyes are open and perception is acute. EMET's "the seeing" is too general. PNEUMA's "those who see clearly" is accurate but verbose. For *visallef*, all three agree on "perverts" -- the piel of *salaf* ("to twist, overturn").

> **[fn9] v.9 -- *Nefesh ha-ger*: "the soul of the sojourner"** -- EMET renders "the soul of the sojourner." LOGOS renders "the soul of the sojourner." PNEUMA renders "what it feels like to be a stranger." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "the soul of the sojourner" -- preserving both *nefesh* ("soul, inner being") and *ger* ("sojourner"). PNEUMA's dynamic rendering is evocative but eliminates both key Hebrew terms. The *ger* is rendered "sojourner" throughout (not PNEUMA's "stranger"), consistent with the convention established in 22:21. The experiential force of *yeda'tem* ("you know") is preserved -- Israel's knowledge of alienation is not theoretical but lived.

> **[fn10] v.10 -- *Tevu'atah*: "yield" vs. "produce"** -- EMET renders "its yield." LOGOS renders "its produce." PNEUMA renders "its produce." The consensus follows EMET -- "its yield" -- as a more precise rendering of *tevu'atah* (from *bo*, "to come in"), which denotes what the land brings forth. "Produce" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is acceptable but more generic.

> **[fn11] v.11 -- *Tishmetennah u-netashtah*: "release" and "abandon"** -- EMET renders "you shall release it and you shall abandon it." LOGOS renders "you shall let it rest and let it lie fallow." PNEUMA renders "let it rest and lie fallow." The consensus follows EMET -- "you shall release it and you shall abandon it" -- preserving the two distinct Hebrew verbs: *shamat* ("to release, let drop") and *natash* ("to abandon, leave"). LOGOS/PNEUMA's "rest" and "lie fallow" are interpretive glosses that lose the active, legal force of the Hebrew. The verb *shamat* is the root of *shemittah* ("release") -- the technical term for the sabbatical year. For the remainder of the verse, EMET's "their remainder the beast of the field shall eat" is preferred over LOGOS's "what they leave over the beasts of the field shall eat" because it preserves the Hebrew word order and the singular collective *khayyat* ("beast of").

> **[fn12] v.12 -- *Yinnafesh*: "may be refreshed"** -- All three translators agree: "may be refreshed" (EMET/LOGOS), "may be refreshed" (PNEUMA). The consensus preserves this rendering. The niphal of *nafash* (denominative from *nefesh*, "soul/breath") literally means "may re-soul himself" -- one of the most remarkable verbs in the Torah, grounding physical rest in the renewal of the life-force. For *tishbot*, EMET's "you shall cease" is preferred over LOGOS's "you shall rest" and PNEUMA's "you must stop" because the root *shavat* means "to cease" (from work), not merely "to rest" -- it is the verbal root of "Sabbath."

> **[fn13] v.13 -- *Tishhameru*: "you shall be watchful" vs. "take heed" vs. "pay careful attention"** -- EMET renders "you shall be watchful." LOGOS renders "take heed." PNEUMA renders "pay careful attention." The consensus follows EMET -- "you shall be watchful" -- as the most precise rendering of the niphal of *shamar* ("to guard oneself, be on guard"). LOGOS's "take heed" and PNEUMA's "pay careful attention" are acceptable but less etymologically precise. For *lo tazkiru*, "you shall not mention" is agreed by all three; the hiphil of *zakhar* means "to cause to be remembered" = "to invoke, mention."

> **[fn14] v.14 -- *Regalim*: "times" vs. "pilgrimage-feast"** -- EMET renders "three times you shall make a pilgrimage-feast." LOGOS renders "three times you shall keep a feast." PNEUMA renders "three times a year you are to celebrate a festival." The consensus follows EMET -- "three times you shall make a pilgrimage-feast" -- because *regalim* (literally "feet") specifically denotes foot-pilgrimages, and *takhog* (from *khagag*, "to celebrate a pilgrimage-feast, to dance") carries the sense of festive pilgrimage. LOGOS's "keep a feast" loses the pilgrimage dimension. PNEUMA's "celebrate a festival" is generic. The word "times" for *regalim* is used in the consensus rendering but the verb *takhog* is rendered "make a pilgrimage-feast" to preserve the full force of the Hebrew.

> **[fn15] v.15 -- *Reiqam*: "empty-handed" vs. "empty"** -- EMET renders "none shall appear before my face empty." LOGOS renders "none shall appear before My face empty-handed." PNEUMA renders "no one is to appear before me empty-handed." The consensus follows LOGOS's "empty-handed" rather than EMET's bare "empty" because *reiqam* in this context specifically means without an offering -- "empty-handed" makes the sense clear without being interpretive. For *ha-Aviv*, "the month of Aviv" is agreed by all three; the consensus does not substitute "Abib" (LOGOS's alternate spelling).

> **[fn16] v.16 -- *Ha-qatsir* and *ha-asif*: festival names** -- All three translators agree: "the Feast of Harvest" and "the Feast of Ingathering." The consensus preserves these renderings. EMET's overall verse structure is followed as the most literal.

> **[fn17] v.17 -- *Ha-Adon YHWH*: "the Lord YHWH"** -- EMET renders "before the face of the Lord, YHWH." LOGOS renders "before the Lord YHWH." PNEUMA renders "before the Lord YHWH." The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA -- "before the face of the Lord YHWH" -- combining EMET's "before the face of" (preserving *el-penei*) with the standard rendering of *ha-Adon YHWH* as "the Lord YHWH." EMET's comma between "Lord" and "YHWH" is removed; both titles form a single designation.

> **[fn18] v.18 -- *Lo tizbach al khamets dam zivkhi*: "slaughter upon leavened bread" vs. "offer with leavened bread"** -- EMET renders "you shall not slaughter upon leavened bread the blood of my sacrifice." LOGOS renders "you shall not offer the blood of My sacrifice with leavened bread." PNEUMA renders "you must not offer the blood of my sacrifice along with anything leavened." The consensus follows EMET -- "you shall not slaughter" for *lo tizbach* (from *zavakh*, "to slaughter [a sacrifice]") and "upon leavened bread" for *al khamets*, preserving both the specific verb and the preposition. LOGOS's "offer" is less precise than "slaughter." LOGOS's "with" and PNEUMA's "along with" weaken the spatial force of *al* ("upon, over"). PNEUMA adds "anything" -- not in the Hebrew.

> **[fn19] v.19 -- *Reshit bikkurei*: "the first of the firstfruits"; *lo tevashel gedi bakhalev immo*: "you shall not boil a kid in the milk of its mother"** -- EMET renders "the first of the firstfruits." LOGOS renders "the best of the firstfruits." PNEUMA renders "the very best of the firstfruits." The consensus follows EMET -- "the first of the firstfruits" -- because *reshit* means "first, beginning" (from *rosh*, "head"), not "best." LOGOS/PNEUMA's "best" is an interpretive extension; while the firstfruits were indeed the best, the Hebrew specifically says "first." For the kid-in-milk prohibition, all three translators agree: "you shall not boil a kid in the milk of its mother." PNEUMA adds "young goat" for *gedi* -- acceptable but unnecessary; "kid" is the standard English term for a young goat and preserves the Hebrew's brevity. The consensus retains "kid."

> **[fn20] v.20 -- *Malakh*: "angel" and *hakhinoti*: "I have prepared"** -- All three translators agree on "angel" for *malakh* and on the overall verse structure. EMET's rendering is followed as the most literal. PNEUMA omits "behold" (*hinneh*) -- the consensus restores it, as *hinneh* marks a dramatic shift in the discourse from legislation to promissory speech.

> **[fn21] v.21 -- *Shemi beqirbo*: "my name is in his midst"** -- EMET renders "my name is in his midst." LOGOS renders "My name is in his midst." PNEUMA renders "my Name is in him." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "my name is in his midst" -- preserving the spatial metaphor of *beqirbo* ("in his interior, in his midst"). PNEUMA's "in him" collapses the spatial dimension; the Hebrew is not merely *bo* ("in him") but *beqirbo* ("in his inner being, in his midst"), which conveys a deeper indwelling. For *al tammer*, EMET's "do not be rebellious" is preferred over LOGOS's "do not rebel" and PNEUMA's "do not defy" -- the Hebrew *tammer* (hiphil of *marah*, "to be rebellious, bitter") describes an ongoing disposition of rebellion, not merely a single act. The LXX renders *beqirbo* as *ep' auto* ("upon him"), flattening the spatial metaphor from indwelling to investiture.

> **[fn22] v.22 -- *Shamo'a tishma*: infinitive absolute** -- EMET renders "if you surely listen." LOGOS renders "if you indeed listen." PNEUMA renders "if you truly listen." The consensus follows EMET -- "if you surely listen" -- preserving the infinitive absolute doubling (*shamo'a tishma*, "hearing you shall hear") per binding convention. LOGOS's "indeed" and PNEUMA's "truly" are acceptable variants. Note the deliberate blurring of identity between the angel and YHWH: Israel is to listen to the angel's voice (*beqolo*, v. 22a), but YHWH says "all that I speak" (*kol asher adabber*, v. 22b).

> **[fn23] v.23 -- *Ve-hikhakhadtiv*: "I will annihilate them" vs. "I will cut them off" vs. "I will wipe them out"** -- EMET renders "I will annihilate them." LOGOS renders "I will cut them off." PNEUMA renders "I will wipe them out." The consensus follows EMET -- "I will annihilate them" -- because the hiphil of *kakhad* ("to hide, destroy") conveys total destruction/removal, not merely cutting off. LOGOS's "cut them off" suggests *karat* rather than *kakhad*. PNEUMA's "wipe them out" is colloquial. Six nations are listed here (cf. seven in Deut 7:1).

> **[fn24] v.24 -- Double infinitive absolute: *haros teharsenem* and *shabber teshabber*: "surely tear down" and "surely break"** -- EMET renders "tearing down you shall tear them down and breaking you shall break their pillars." LOGOS renders "you shall utterly tear them down and you shall utterly break their pillars." PNEUMA renders "you must tear them down completely and smash their sacred pillars to pieces." The consensus follows a blend: "you shall surely tear them down and you shall surely break their pillars" -- preserving both infinitive absolute constructions per binding convention with "surely" rather than EMET's participle-based doubling or LOGOS's "utterly." PNEUMA adds "sacred" before "pillars" and "to pieces" -- neither is in the Hebrew. The *mattsevot* ("standing stones") were cult objects, but the Hebrew does not specify them as "sacred."

> **[fn25] v.25 -- Person shift: "he will bless" / "I will turn aside"** -- EMET renders "he will bless your bread and your water, and I will turn aside sickness from your midst." LOGOS renders "He will bless your bread and your water, and I will remove sickness from your midst." PNEUMA renders "he will bless your food and your water. I will remove sickness from among you." The consensus follows EMET -- "he will bless...I will turn aside" -- preserving the remarkable person shift in the Hebrew (third-person *u-verakh*, "he will bless," shifting to first-person *va-hasiroti*, "I will turn aside"). EMET's "turn aside" for *va-hasiroti* (hiphil of *sur*, "to turn aside, remove") is more precise than LOGOS/PNEUMA's "remove." PNEUMA's "food" for *lakhem* ("bread") loses specificity.

> **[fn26] v.26 -- *Meshakkelah* and *aqarah*: "one who miscarries" and "one who is barren"** -- EMET renders "one who miscarries or one who is barren." LOGOS renders "a woman who miscarries or is barren." PNEUMA renders "no woman in your land will miscarry or be barren." The consensus follows EMET -- "there shall not be one who miscarries or one who is barren" -- because the Hebrew uses feminine participle and adjective without specifying "woman" (*ishah*). LOGOS adds "woman" interpretively. PNEUMA restructures entirely. For *amalle* ("I will fill"), the consensus follows EMET's "I will fill" over LOGOS's "I will fulfill" -- the piel of *male* means specifically "to fill," and the promise is that YHWH will fill out the full measure of days.

> **[fn27] v.27 -- *Oref*: "the back of the neck" vs. "those who turn the back"** -- EMET renders "I will give all your enemies to you as the back of the neck." LOGOS renders "I will give all your enemies to you as those who turn the back." PNEUMA renders "I will make all your enemies turn their backs to you in flight." The consensus follows EMET -- "the back of the neck" -- preserving the Hebrew noun *oref* ("back of the neck, nape") without interpretive expansion. The image is of enemies turning their backs in flight, but the Hebrew states the metaphor directly: YHWH gives the enemy as "neck" (i.e., presenting only their fleeing backs). LOGOS and PNEUMA clarify the image but lose the Hebrew's compressed force.

> **[fn28] v.28 -- *Ha-tsir'ah*: "the hornet"** -- All three translators agree: "the hornet." The consensus preserves this rendering. The definite article suggests a known divine instrument. Whether literal (stinging insects), metaphorical (panic, plague, or Egyptian military campaigns), or theological (divine terror made zoological) has been debated since antiquity. The LXX pluralizes to *sphekias* ("wasps").

> **[fn29] v.29 -- *Shemamah*: "desolation" vs. "desolate"** -- EMET renders "lest the land become a desolation." LOGOS renders "lest the land become desolate." PNEUMA renders "or the land would become desolate." The consensus follows EMET -- "a desolation" -- because the Hebrew *shemamah* is a noun ("a desolation, a waste"), not an adjective. The land does not merely become "desolate" (an adjectival state) but becomes "a desolation" (a substantive condition).

> **[fn30] v.30 -- *Me'at me'at*: "little by little"; *tifreh*: "you are fruitful"** -- All three translators agree: "little by little." For *tifreh* (qal of *parah*, "to be fruitful"), EMET renders "you are fruitful," LOGOS renders "you are fruitful," PNEUMA renders "you have increased enough." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "you are fruitful" -- preserving the verb's echo of the creation blessing (*peru u-revu*, Gen 1:28). PNEUMA's "increased enough" adds a qualifier not in the Hebrew.

> **[fn31] v.31 -- *Yam-Suf*: "Sea of Reeds" (binding convention); *ha-nahar*: "the River"** -- EMET renders "Sea of Reeds." LOGOS renders "Sea of Reeds." PNEUMA renders "Sea of Reeds." All three follow the binding convention. The consensus preserves "the River" for *ha-nahar* (the Euphrates) without identifying it by modern name, following EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "Euphrates" -- accurate but not in the Hebrew. The four boundaries: Sea of Reeds (south), Sea of the Philistines (west/Mediterranean), the wilderness (south-southwest), the River (northeast/Euphrates). The LXX renders *Yam-Suf* as *erythra thalassa* ("Red Sea") -- the well-known mistranslation that entered Western tradition.

> **[fn32] v.32 -- *Lo tikhrot lahem ve-le-eloheihem berit*: "you shall not cut for them or for their gods a covenant"** -- EMET renders "you shall not cut for them or for their gods a covenant." LOGOS renders "you shall not make a covenant with them or with their gods." PNEUMA renders "you must not make a covenant with them or with their gods." The consensus follows EMET -- preserving "cut" for *tikhrot* (from *karat*, "to cut"), which reflects the ancient covenant-making ceremony of cutting sacrificial animals (cf. Gen 15:10). LOGOS/PNEUMA's "make" loses this ceremonial resonance. EMET's word order, placing "a covenant" at the end, follows the Hebrew.

> **[fn33] v.33 -- *Moqesh*: "snare" vs. "trap"** -- EMET renders "it will be a snare to you." LOGOS renders "a snare to you." PNEUMA renders "a trap for you." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "a snare" for *moqesh* (from *yaqash*, "to lay a snare"). PNEUMA's "trap" is acceptable but less specific; *moqesh* evokes a bird-trap that catches by surprise, not a general trap. The entire Book of the Covenant ends on this warning: serving other gods will become an inescapable snare. The LXX renders *moqesh* as *proskomma* ("stumbling block") -- a different metaphor.

---

## Translator's Notes

### Structural Note

Exodus 23 concludes the Book of the Covenant (*Sefer ha-Berit*) that began at 20:22 (or 21:1 for the casuistic legislation), forming the final chapter of the legal corpus given to Moses at Sinai. The chapter divides into four major sections, each with a distinct legal and theological character:

**Section I: Justice and Integrity (vv. 1-9)** -- Apodictic commands addressed to the individual Israelite as participant in the judicial process: as witness (v. 1), as member of the assembly (v. 2), as judge (vv. 3, 6-8), and as neighbor of the sojourner (v. 9). Verses 4-5 interrupt the court-law sequence with two case-laws about helping an enemy's animal -- a striking intrusion of personal ethics into the judicial context. The section is framed by the sojourner motif: v. 9 recalls the *ger*-command of 22:21, forming an inclusio. The pairing of v. 3 (do not favor the poor) and v. 6 (do not pervert the poor man's justice) creates a frame of absolute impartiality.

**Section II: Sabbatical Rhythms (vv. 10-13)** -- The seventh-year land-rest (vv. 10-11) and the seventh-day rest (v. 12), followed by a comprehensive warning against invoking the names of other gods (v. 13). The sabbatical institutions create a temporal architecture of covenant life at both the weekly and septennial scale. The purpose clauses are notable: the land-rest benefits the poor and wildlife (v. 11); the weekly rest benefits animals, slaves, and sojourners (v. 12). The rare verb *yinnafesh* ("may be re-souled," v. 12) grounds physical rest in the renewal of the *nefesh*.

**Section III: The Three Pilgrimage Festivals (vv. 14-19)** -- The most concise festival calendar in the Torah: Unleavened Bread (*matstsot*), Harvest (*qatsir*, later Shavuot/Weeks), and Ingathering (*asif*, later Sukkot/Tabernacles). The agricultural orientation is conspicuous: the festivals are tied to the rhythm of planting, first-harvest, and final ingathering. The section concludes with the kid-in-milk prohibition (v. 19b), whose placement within the festival legislation suggests a cultic rather than purely dietary context.

**Section IV: The Angel and the Conquest (vv. 20-33)** -- The theological capstone of the entire Book of the Covenant. YHWH announces the sending of his angel (vv. 20-23), commands the destruction of Canaanite cult objects (v. 24), promises blessings of sustenance, health, fertility, and military victory (vv. 25-28), describes the gradual process of conquest (vv. 29-30), and establishes territorial boundaries (v. 31). The section closes with a prohibition against covenants with the Canaanites and their gods (vv. 32-33). The conditional structure -- obedience yields blessing, disobedience yields the snare -- establishes the covenantal pattern that will govern the rest of Israel's story.

### Key Decisions

**"Lift up" for *tissa* (v. 1):** The verb *nasa* ("to lift, carry") applied to a report (*shema*) means to pick up and circulate -- to give currency to a rumor. The same verb appears in the Decalogue's *lo tissa et-shem YHWH le-shav* ("you shall not lift up the name of YHWH in vain," 20:7). The verbal echo links courtroom falsehood to profanation of the divine name.

**"Report of emptiness" for *shema shav* (v. 1):** "Emptiness" is preserved rather than "false" because *shav* carries a broader semantic range -- emptiness, worthlessness, vanity -- that "false" does not fully capture.

**"Surely loosen" for *azov ta'azov* (v. 5):** The unusual meaning of *azav* as "to loosen, release" (rather than the common "to abandon") is preserved, following the contextual sense of releasing a burden from a collapsed animal.

**"Be refreshed" / "re-souled" for *yinnafesh* (v. 12):** The niphal of *nafash*, denominative from *nefesh* ("soul/breath"), is rendered "be refreshed" -- with the understanding that the Hebrew literally means "to restore one's *nefesh*."

**"My name is in his midst" for *shemi beqirbo* (v. 21):** The spatial metaphor of *beqirbo* ("in his interior/midst") is preserved rather than flattened to "in him." YHWH's name indwells the angel, making the angel a bearer of divine presence and authority.

**"Annihilate" for *ve-hikhakhadtiv* (v. 23):** The hiphil of *kakhad* ("to hide, efface, destroy") conveys total removal -- stronger than "cut off" and more precise than "wipe out."

**"Sea of Reeds" for *Yam-Suf* (v. 31):** Per binding convention. The Hebrew *suf* means "reed" (cf. the *suf* in which baby Moses's basket was placed, Exod 2:3), not "red."

**"Snare" for *moqesh* (v. 33):** The specific image of a bird-snare is preserved. The Book of the Covenant ends with this warning: serving other gods is not a frontal assault but a hidden trap that catches the unwary.

### Vaticanus (LXX) Divergences

The Vaticanus text of Exodus 23 (pp. 65-66) preserves several notable divergences from the MT: (1) In v. 2, the LXX clarifies the notoriously difficult Hebrew syntax with a more transparent prohibition against siding with the majority to pervert justice (*ou prosthese meta plethous ekklinai krisin*). (2) In v. 3, the MT's *tehdar* ("favor, honor") is rendered *eleeseis* ("show pity/mercy") -- a stronger emotional term. (3) In v. 5, the LXX significantly expands the compressed Hebrew, making the obligation to assist the enemy's fallen donkey more explicit. (4) In v. 8, the MT's *piqqekhim* ("the clear-sighted") is rendered *bleponton* ("those who see"). (5) In v. 11, the sabbath-year legislation is rendered with *aphesis* ("release"), the same term used for the Jubilee. (6) In v. 17, *ha-Adon YHWH* ("the Lord YHWH") is rendered *kuriou tou theou sou* ("the Lord your God"), with the characteristic Septuagintal substitution of *kurios* for YHWH. (7) In v. 19, the LXX substitutes *arna* ("lamb") for the MT's *gedi* ("kid/young goat") -- a notable lexical variant. (8) In v. 21, *beqirbo* ("in his midst") is rendered *ep' auto* ("upon him"), flattening the spatial metaphor from indwelling to investiture. (9) In v. 28, *ha-tsir'ah* ("the hornet") is rendered *sphekias* ("wasps"), pluralizing the collective singular. (10) In v. 31, *Yam-Suf* is rendered *erythra thalassa* ("Red Sea") -- the well-known mistranslation that entered Western tradition; the Hebrew means "Sea of Reeds."

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A) with reference to Codex Vaticanus (LXX). All transliterations follow standard academic conventions. Hebrew text follows the Masoretic vocalization.*
